revision.text:
[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō und Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲}}) or „Gaya armor“, „Silla armor“ is a form of [[Japanese armor]], that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == The ''Tankō'' (literally „little bowl“) is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Jòdai no Katchù (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao)) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''Tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[Rivet|riveted]] side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate, as to provide protection for the neck. The front side was had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders. It is believed that the ''Tankō'' form of armor was influenced by Chinese infantry armor of the time.
== Keikō == [[File:KofunCuirass2.jpg|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''Keikō'' (literally "hanging shell") was influenced by the protective clothing of mounted nomads in northern China. The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces, as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref>. The difference between the ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' lies only in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''Keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, ISBN 1-85532-131-9, S. 28–29.</ref>.
== Helms == [[File:KofunHelmetIronAndGiltCopper5thCenturyIseProvince.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (jap. Mabisashi)<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, ISBN 91-7146-193-0, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''Tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''Tankō'' chest piece)<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and is decorated with triangular and linear patterns<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, ISBN 1-873410-13-1, S. 1.</ref>.
== Naming Origins == The names ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, ISBN 4-06-206489-8. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, ISBN 0-8248-1966-7. * Karl F. Friday: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, ISBN 0-415-32962-0, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, ISBN 978-1-4027-6312-0.
== References == <references />
== Weblinks == * {{commonscat|Tanko}} * [http://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Rekonstruktion einer Tankō] * [http://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofunjidai Tankō bei Flickr]. * [http://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofunjidai Tankō im japanischen Nationalmuseum Tokio bei Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York]
[[Category:Japanese armour]]

September 2011)]</ref>. Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. == Helms == [[File:KofunHelmetIronAndGiltCopper5thCenturyIseProvince.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (jap. Mabisashi)<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''Tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''Tankō'' chest piece)<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and is decorated with triangular and linear patterns<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>.
== Naming Origins == The names ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.

Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * Karl F. Friday: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.

[[Category:Japanese armour]]

[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō und Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲}}) or „Gaya armor“, „Silla armor“ is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == The ''Tankō'' (literally "little bowl“) is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as [[Japanese language|Jap.]] Jòdai no Katchù (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''Tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[Rivet|riveted]] side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders. It is believed that the ''Tankō'' form of armor was influenced by Chinese infantry armor of the time.
== Keikō == [[File:KofunCuirass2.jpg|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''Keikō'' (literally "hanging shell") was influenced by the protective clothing of mounted nomads in northern China. The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces, as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref>. The difference between the ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' lies only in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''Keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>.
== Helms == [[File:KofunHelmetIronAndGiltCopper5thCenturyIseProvince.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (jap. Mabisashi)<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''Tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''Tankō'' chest piece)<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and is decorated with triangular and linear patterns<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>.
== Naming Origins == The names ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.

== Naming Origins == The names ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * Karl F. Friday: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.
== References == <references />
== Weblinks == {{commonscat|Tanko}} * [http://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Reconstruction of a Tankō helm] * [http://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofun Jidai Tankō from Flickr]. * [http://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofun Jidai Tankō in the Tokyo National Musuem from Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun helm in Metropolitan Museum, New York]
[[Category:Japanese armour]]

[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō und Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲}}) or "Gaya armor“, "Silla armor“ is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == The ''Tankō'' (literally "little bowl“) is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''Tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[Rivet|riveted]] side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders. It is believed that the ''Tankō'' form of armor was influenced by Chinese infantry armor of the time.
== Keikō == [[File:KofunCuirass2.jpg|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''Keikō'' (literally "hanging shell") was influenced by the protective clothing of mounted nomads in northern China. The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref>. The difference between the ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''Keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>.
== Helms == [[File:KofunHelmetIronAndGiltCopper5thCenturyIseProvince.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (Jap. Mabisashi)<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''Tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''Tankō'' chest piece)<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>.
== Naming Origins == The names ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * Karl F. Friday: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.
== References == <references />
== Weblinks == {{commonscat|Tanko}} * [http://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Reconstruction of a Tankō helm] * [http://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofun Jidai Tankō from Flickr]. * [http://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofun Jidai Tankō in the Tokyo National Musuem from Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun helm in Metropolitan Museum, New York]
[[Category:Japanese armour]]

== Tankō == The ''Tankō'' (literally "little bowl“) is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''Tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[Rivet|riveted]] side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders. It is believed that the ''Tankō'' form of armor was influenced by Chinese infantry armor of the time.
== Keikō == [[File:KofunCuirass2.jpg|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''Keikō'' (literally "hanging shell") was influenced by the protective clothing of mounted nomads in northern China. The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref>. The difference between the ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''Keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>.
== Helms == [[File:KofunHelmetIronAndGiltCopper5thCenturyIseProvince.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (Jap. Mabisashi)<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''Tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''Tankō'' chest piece)<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref>. The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>.
== Naming Origins == The names ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * Karl F. Friday: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.
== References == <references />
== Weblinks == {{commonscat|Tanko}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Reconstruction of a Tankō helm] * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofun Jidai Tankō from Flickr]. * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofun Jidai Tankō in the Tokyo National Museum from Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun helm in Metropolitan Museum, New York]
[[Category:Japanese armour]]

September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''Tankō'' and ''Keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * Karl F. Friday: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.
== References == <references />
== External links == {{commons category|Tanko}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Reconstruction of a Tankō helm] * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofun Jidai Tankō from Flickr]. * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofun Jidai Tankō in the Tokyo National Museum from Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun helm in Metropolitan Museum, New York]

revision.comment: format, translation for tanko way off
revision.text:
[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō and Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲}} "short armor") is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == The ''tankō'' is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[rivet]]ed side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders.
== Keikō == [[File:KofunCuirass2.jpg|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''keikō'' (literally "hanging shell"). The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance.<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref> The difference between the ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps.<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>
== Helms == [[File:KofunHelmetIronAndGiltCopper5thCenturyIseProvince.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (''mabisashi'')<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''tankō'' chest piece).<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * Karl F. Friday: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.
== References == <references />
== External links == {{commons category|Tanko}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Reconstruction of a Tankō helm] * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofun Jidai Tankō from Flickr]. * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofun Jidai Tankō in the Tokyo National Museum from Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun helm in Metropolitan Museum, New York]
[[Category:Japanese armour]]

[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō and Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲}} "short armor") is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == [[File:Tanko-Armor-Kofun-Period-by-Sugiyama-Sueo.png|thumb|Tanko Armor by Sugiyama Sueo]] The ''tankō'' is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[rivet]]ed side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders.
== Keikō == [[File:KofunCuirass2.jpg|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''keikō'' ("hanging armor"). The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance.<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref> The difference between the ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps.<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>
== Helmet == [[File:KofunHelmetIronAndGiltCopper5thCenturyIseProvince.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (''mabisashi'')<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''tankō'' chest piece).<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * [[Karl Friday]]: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.

Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>
== Helmet == [[File:Helmet MET DT305558.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (''mabisashi'')<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''tankō'' chest piece).<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * [[Karl Friday]]: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.
== References == <references />
== External links == {{commons category|Tanko}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Reconstruction of a Tankō helm] * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofun Jidai Tankō from Flickr]. * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofun Jidai Tankō in the Tokyo National Museum from Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun helm in Metropolitan Museum, New York]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō and Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲}} "short armor") is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == [[File:Tanko-Armor-Kofun-Period-by-Sugiyama-Sueo.png|thumb|Tanko Armor by Sugiyama Sueo]] The ''tankō'' is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[rivet]]ed side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders.
== Keikō == [[File:Iron Tanko Armor, Kofun period, 5th century, from Tsukando Tumulus, Yoshii-machi, Ukiha-shi, Fukuoka, sewn with leather strings - Tokyo National Museum - DSC06395.JPG|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''keikō'' ("hanging armor"). The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance.<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref> The difference between the ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps.<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>
== Helmet == [[File:Helmet MET DT305558.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (''mabisashi'')<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''tankō'' chest piece).<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * [[Karl Friday]]: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.
== References == <references />
== External links == {{commons category|Tanko}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Reconstruction of a Tankō helm] * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofun Jidai Tankō from Flickr]. * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofun Jidai Tankō in the Tokyo National Museum from Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun helm in Metropolitan Museum, New York]

[[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * [[Karl Friday]]: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.

[[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Japanese armour]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tankō]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:転送]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tankō}}

[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō and Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲}} "short armor") is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == [[File:Tanko-Armor-Kofun-Period-by-Sugiyama-Sueo.png|thumb|Tanko Armor by Sugiyama Sueo]] The ''tankō'' is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[rivet]]ed side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders.
== Keikō == [[File:Iron Tanko Armor, Kofun period, 5th century, from Tsukando Tumulus, Yoshii-machi, Ukiha-shi, Fukuoka, sewn with leather strings - Tokyo National Museum - DSC06395.JPG|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''keikō'' ("hanging armor"). The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance.<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref> The difference between the ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps.<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>
== Helmet == [[File:Helmet MET DT305558.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (''mabisashi'')<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''tankō'' chest piece).<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * [[Karl Friday]]: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.

[[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Japanese armour]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tankō]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:redirect]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tankō]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tankō}}

[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō and Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲}} "short armor") is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == [[File:Tanko-Armor-Kofun-Period-by-Sugiyama-Sueo.png|thumb|Tanko Armor by Sugiyama Sueo]] The ''tankō'' is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[rivet]]ed side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders.
== Keikō == [[File:Iron Tanko Armor, Kofun period, 5th century, from Tsukando Tumulus, Yoshii-machi, Ukiha-shi, Fukuoka, sewn with leather strings - Tokyo National Museum - DSC06395.JPG|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''keikō'' ("hanging armor"). The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance.<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref> The difference between the ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps.<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>
== Helmet == [[File:Helmet MET DT305558.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (''mabisashi'')<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''tankō'' chest piece).<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * [[Karl Friday]]: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.

[[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Japanese armour]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tankō]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tankō]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tankō}} [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]]

[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō and Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲|1=Tankō}} "short armor") is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == [[File:Tanko-Armor-Kofun-Period-by-Sugiyama-Sueo.png|thumb|Tanko Armor by Sugiyama Sueo]] The ''tankō'' is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[rivet]]ed side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders.
== Keikō == [[File:Iron Tanko Armor, Kofun period, 5th century, from Tsukando Tumulus, Yoshii-machi, Ukiha-shi, Fukuoka, sewn with leather strings - Tokyo National Museum - DSC06395.JPG|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''keikō'' ("hanging armor"). The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance.<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref> The difference between the ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps.<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>
== Helmet == [[File:Helmet MET DT305558.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (''mabisashi'')<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''tankō'' chest piece).<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * [[Karl Friday]]: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.

[[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:Japanese armour]] [[Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tankō]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tankō]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tankō}} [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tankō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

[[File:KofunCuirass.jpg|thumbnail|Tankō and Helm ([[Kabuto]]), displayed in the [[Tokyo National Museum]].]] '''Tankō''' ([[Japanese writing system|Jap.]] {{lang|ja|短甲|1=Tankō}} "short armor") is a form of [[Japanese armor]] that was common in the [[Kofun period]].
== Tankō == [[File:Tanko-Armor-Kofun-Period-by-Sugiyama-Sueo.png|thumb|Tanko Armor by Sugiyama Sueo]] The ''tankō'' is the first uniquely definable type of Japanese armor. Other types that were used earlier such as ''Jòdai no Katchù'' (ancient armor, as described by armor historian Suenaga Masao) have been mentioned, but are not entirely verifiable. The ''tankō'' was made of sheet iron and [[Tanning (leather)|tanned leather]], laced at the hips to conform to the wearer's body. The leather served as a support for the iron sheets, and as a protective [[Lining (sewing)|lining]] meant to prevent damage to the wearer's clothing and skin. The iron sheets were formed into strips that were [[rivet]]ed side-by-side or overlapping. The carapace was built higher than the breastplate in order to provide protection for the neck. The front side had an opening that could be closed once the armor was put on. The armor was attached to the body via fabric bands that ran over the shoulders.
== Keikō == [[File:Iron Tanko Armor, Kofun period, 5th century, from Tsukando Kofun, Yoshii-machi, Ukiha-shi, Fukuoka, sewn with leather strings - Tokyo National Museum - DSC06395.JPG|thumbnail|Keikō armor]] Another Japanese armor type from the Kofun period, the ''keikō'' ("hanging armor"). The type was also used in Japan as armor for mounted forces as the use of cavalry in warfare increased in importance.<ref name="Farris 76" >William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures.'' 1998, S. 76.</ref> The difference between the ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' lies in the upper chest area of the armor, which in the ''keikō'' variant included a protective sheath of iron bands. The lower portion of both types consisted of overlapping iron [[Lamellar armour|lammellae]]. The use of leather underpinnings was also used by both forms. The bands were fixed together through riveting or the use of leather straps.<ref>Anthony J. Bryant: ''Early Samurai. 200–1500 AD'' (= ''Osprey Military. Elite Series'' 35). Colour Plates by Angus McBride. Osprey Publishing, London 1991, {{ISBN|1-85532-131-9}}, S. 28–29.</ref>
== Helmet == [[File:Helmet MET DT305558.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (1st version) in Metropolitan Museum, New York]] The helmets (''mabisashi'')<ref>Lars Vargö: '' Social and economic conditions for the formation of the early Japanese state'' (= ''Japanological Studies.'' Vol. 1). Stockholm University, Institute of Oriental Languages, Dept. of Japanese and Korean, Stockholm 1982, {{ISBN|91-7146-193-0}}, S. 26.</ref> worn with the ''tankō'' armor type are known in two versions. The first type ([[Japanese language|jap.]] Kondu Maruhachi) consisted of sheets of overlapping iron similar to the ''tankō'' chest piece).<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_kondu_maruhachi/objectview.aspx?page=216&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=216&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40001236&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> Lamella pieces were riveted to horizontally lying bands to create a dome shape for the helmet. A short eye shield was attached to the front, which was often pierced with holes. A small hump with a fixing pin for a crest lied at the top of the helmet's dome. [[File:KofunHelmet.jpg|thumbnail|Kofun helm (2nd version)]] The second type was created differently, with horizontal and vertical bands throughout the helm's dome.<ref>[http://www.metmuseum.org/works_of_art/collection_database/arms_and_armor/helmet_hachi/objectview_enlarge.aspx?page=116&sort=0&sortdir=asc&keyword=&fp=116&dd1=4&dd2=0&vw=1&collID=4&OID=40000659&vT=1&hi=0&ov=0 Kofun-Helm im Metropolitan Museum, New York, online visible, (viewed on 23. September 2011)]</ref> The eye shield and attachment for the crest were much larger. The attachment also included a shell-like form that served to further fasten and accentuate the crest.
== Shield == The shield worn with this type of armor was made of sheet iron fixed to a wooden base. The shield's components were fixed to each other and the shield's body through the use of riveting. The exterior has a slightly convex shape, and was decorated with triangular and linear patterns.<ref>Catharina Blomberg: ''The heart of the warrior. Origins and religious background of the Samurai system in feudal Japan.'' Japan Library, Sandgate 1994, {{ISBN|1-873410-13-1}}, S. 1.</ref>
== Naming Origins == The names ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are not historical, but [[Archaeology|archaeological]] in origin. In the sacrificial book of [[Tōdai-ji]] from 756 ''tankō'' and ''keikō'' are listed as gifts. These terms were then assigned to varying types of excavated armor.
== Literature == * 講談社, ''Japan. An illustrated encyclopedia.'' Band 1: ''A – L.'' Kodansha, Tokio 1993, {{ISBN|4-06-206489-8}}. * William Wayne Farris: ''Sacred texts and buried treasures. Issues in the historical archaeology of ancient Japan.'' University of Hawai'i Press, Honolulu HI 1998, {{ISBN|0-8248-1966-7}}. * [[Karl Friday]]: ''Samurai, warfare and the state in early medieval Japan.'' Routledge, New York NY u. a. 2004, {{ISBN|0-415-32962-0}}, S. 186, 187. * Thomas Louis, Tommy Ito: ''Samurai. The Code of the Warrior.'' Sterling Publishing Company, New York NY 2008, {{ISBN|978-1-4027-6312-0}}.
== References == <references />
== External links == {{commons category|Tanko}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20111007232245/http://img279.echo.cx/img279/1605/009a1ik.gif Reconstruction of a Tankō helm] * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423881160/ Kofun Jidai Tankō from Flickr]. * [https://www.flickr.com/photos/93127755@N00/5423880798/ Kofun Jidai Tankō in the Tokyo National Museum from Flickr] * [http://www.metmuseum.org/toah/works-of-art/28.60.2 Kofun helm in Metropolitan Museum, New York]
